subject: Physics
q:
A block of mass 40 kg slides over a surface, when a mass of 4 kg is suspended through an inextensible massless string passing over frictionless pulley as shown below.
The coefficient of kinetic friction between the surface and block is 0.02. The acceleration of block is: (Given g=10 m s-2.)
question:
A block of mass 40 kg slides over a surface, when a mass of 4 kg is suspended through an inextensible massless string passing over frictionless pulley as shown below.
The coefficient of kinetic friction between the surface and block is 0.02. The acceleration of block is: (Given g=10 m s-2.)
solution:
For hanging block, 

40-T=4a     ⋯1

Now, maximum value of friction force will be,

fmax=μN=0.02×40×10=8 N

For block on table, T-8=40a     ⋯2

From equation(1) and equation (2), we get32=44a ⇒a=3244=811 m s-2

subject: Physics
q: A smooth circular groove has a smooth vertical wall as shown in figure. A block of mass m moves against the wall with a speed v. Which of the following curve represents the correct relation between the normal reaction on the block by the wall N and speed of the block v?
question: A smooth circular groove has a smooth vertical wall as shown in figure. A block of mass m moves against the wall with a speed v. Which of the following curve represents the correct relation between the normal reaction on the block by the wall N and speed of the block v?
solution:
The required centripetal force will be provided by the normal reaction

⇒N=mv2r

This equation is similar to y=kx2 which is a parabola.

subject: Physics
q: All surfaces shown in figure are assumed to be frictionless and the pulleys and the string are light. The acceleration of the block of mass 2 kg is:
question: All surfaces shown in figure are assumed to be frictionless and the pulleys and the string are light. The acceleration of the block of mass 2 kg is:
solution:
For 4 kg block:

40-2T=4a⇒T=20-2a

For 2 kg block:

T-10=4a⇒20-2a-10=4a⇒10=6a⇒a=106=g6 m s-2

Acceleration of the block of mass 2 kg, 2a=g3 m s-2.

subject: Physics
q: A car of mass ' $m$ ' moves on a banked road having radius ' $r$ ' and banking angle $\theta$. To avoid slipping from banked road, the maximum permissible speed of the car is $v_0$. The coefficient of friction $\mu$ between the wheels of the car and the banked road is
question: A car of mass ' $m$ ' moves on a banked road having radius ' $r$ ' and banking angle $\theta$. To avoid slipping from banked road, the maximum permissible speed of the car is $v_0$. The coefficient of friction $\mu$ between the wheels of the car and the banked road is
solution: So, $N=m g \cos \theta+\frac{m v_0^2}{r} \sin \theta$ $f_r=\mu m g \cos \theta+\frac{\mu m v_0^2}{r} \sin \theta$ $\begin{aligned} & \text { And } \frac{m v_0^2}{r} \cos \theta=m g \sin \theta+f_r \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{m v_0^2}{r} \cos \theta-m g \sin \theta=\mu\left(m g \cos \theta+\frac{m v_0^2}{r} \sin \theta\right) \\ & \Rightarrow\left(v_0^2-g r \tan \theta\right)=\mu\left(v_0^2 \tan \theta+g r\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mu=\frac{v_0^2-g r \tan \theta}{g r+v_0^2 \tan \theta} \end{aligned}$

T=2πMk

T'=2πMk'

T'=2T

question: A rocket with a lift-off mass $3.5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~kg}$ is blasted upwards with an initial acceleration of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. Then the initial thrust of the blast is
solution: Thrust $=$ Mass $\times$ Acceleration $=3.5 \times 10^4 \times 10=3.5 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N}$

The position of that object at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> after starting from rest. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> will be _____ .
solution:
a→=F→m=10i^+5j^0.1=100i^+50j^

Displacement covered in vector form can be written as, s→=u→t+12a→t2

⇒s→=12100i^+50j^22

, ⇒s→=200i^+100j^

Comparing it with, s→=ai^+bj^, we get a=200 & b=100

Therefore, ab=2

subject: Physics
q:
Consider a block kept on an inclined plane (inclined at 45°) as shown in the figure. If the force required to just push it up the incline is 2 times the force required to just prevent it from sliding down, the coefficient of friction between the block and inclined plane (µ) is equal to :

.
question:
Consider a block kept on an inclined plane (inclined at 45°) as shown in the figure. If the force required to just push it up the incline is 2 times the force required to just prevent it from sliding down, the coefficient of friction between the block and inclined plane (µ) is equal to :

.
solution:
The forces acting on block is shown in above figure.

Here, normal force, N=mgcos45° and frictional force, f=μN.

Balancing the force along incline, we have

F1=mg sin45°+f=mg sin45°+μN

⇒F1=mg2+μmg cos45°

⇒F1=mg21+μ

Now, the forces acting on block to just push it up on inclined plane is shown below.

Balancing the force along incline, we have

F2=mg sin45°-f=mg sin45°-μN

=mg21-μ

Given, F1=2F2

⇒mg21+μ=2mg21-μ

⇒1+μ=2-2μ

⇒μ=13=0.33

subject: Physics
q: A machine gun fires a bullet of mass $40 \mathrm{~g}$ with a velocity $1200 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The man holding it can exert a maximum force of $144 \mathrm{~N}$ on the gun. How many bullets can he fire per second at the most?
question: A machine gun fires a bullet of mass $40 \mathrm{~g}$ with a velocity $1200 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The man holding it can exert a maximum force of $144 \mathrm{~N}$ on the gun. How many bullets can he fire per second at the most?
solution:
Change in momentum for each bullet fired is
$$
=\frac{40}{1000} \times 1200=48 \mathrm{~N}
$$
If a bullet fired exerts a force of $48 \mathrm{~N}$ on man's hand so $\rho$ man can exert maximum force of $144 \mathrm{~N}$, number of bullets that can be fired $=144 / 48=3$ bullets.

subject: Physics
q:
The initial mass of a rocket is 1000 kg. Calculate at what rate the fuel should be burnt so that the rocket is given an acceleration of, 20 m s-2. The gases come out at a relative speed of 500 m s-1, with respect to the rocket:

Use g=10 m s-2
question:
The initial mass of a rocket is 1000 kg. Calculate at what rate the fuel should be burnt so that the rocket is given an acceleration of, 20 m s-2. The gases come out at a relative speed of 500 m s-1, with respect to the rocket:

Use g=10 m s-2
solution:
Here we are applying the equation, Fr−mg=ma

From Newton's second law, Fr=dmdtvr⇒dmdtvr=mg+ma

Put the given values in the above equations, 

⇒dmdt500=1000(10+20)⇒dmdt=60 kg s-1.

question: A spring whose unstrentches length is <math><mi>l</mi></math> has a force constant <math><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The spring is cut into two pieces of unstretches lengths <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> where, <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>n</mi><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><mi>n</mi></math> is an integer. solution:
Spring constants of the spring will be inversely proportional to length.
k1k2=l2l1=1n

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement-I: An elevator can go up or down with uniform speed when its weight is balanced with the tension of its cable.
Statement-II: Force exerted by the floor of an elevator on the foot of a person standing on it is more than his/her weight when the elevator goes down with increasing speed.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement-I: An elevator can go up or down with uniform speed when its weight is balanced with the tension of its cable.
Statement-II: Force exerted by the floor of an elevator on the foot of a person standing on it is more than his/her weight when the elevator goes down with increasing speed.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
When elevator is moving with uniform speed T=Fg, as the net force along the vertical direction should be zero.

Therefore, Statement-I is true.

Now, when elevator is going down with increasing speed, its acceleration is downward.
Hence, 

W-N=ma⇒W-N=Wg×a

⇒N=W1-ag

Clearly, normal reaction is less than weight. So Statement-II is false.

question: Which of the following option correctly describes the variation of the speed <math><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi></math> and acceleration 'a' of a point mass falling vertically in a viscous medium that applies a force <math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">υ</mi></math> , where 'k' is a constant, on the body? solution:
a = (g - α v)

dv dt = g - α v

∫0vdvg-αv=∫0tdt⇒ℓng-αvg=-αt

v=v01-e-αta=dvdt=v0αe-αt=a0e-αt

q: A light string passing over a smooth light fixed pulley connects two blocks of masses m1 and m2. If the acceleration of the system is g8, then the ratio of masses is
question: A light string passing over a smooth light fixed pulley connects two blocks of masses m1 and m2. If the acceleration of the system is g8, then the ratio of masses is
solution:
Acceleration of the system can be written as, a=m1-m2gm1+m2=g8

⇒8m1-8m2=m1+m2

⇒7m1=9m2

⇒m1m2=97

subject: Physics
q: A block takes $t$ time to slide down a plane inclined at $45°$ to the horizontal. If the surface is made smooth (frictionless), the block takes time $\dfrac{t}{2}$ to slide down the plane. The coefficient of friction between the block and the inclined plane is $\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{100}\right)$. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
question: A block takes $t$ time to slide down a plane inclined at $45°$ to the horizontal. If the surface is made smooth (frictionless), the block takes time $\dfrac{t}{2}$ to slide down the plane. The coefficient of friction between the block and the inclined plane is $\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{100}\right)$. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
solution:
Let the length of the inclined plane be $s$.

For the smooth inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_1 = g \sin \theta$.

The time taken is $t_1 = \dfrac{t}{2}$. Using the second equation of motion $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$ with $u = 0$:

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} (g \sin \theta) \left(\dfrac{t}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{8} g \sin \theta t^2$

For the rough inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_2 = g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta)$.

The time taken is $t_2 = t$. Using the equation of motion again:

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta) t^2$

Equating the two expressions for $s$:

$\dfrac{1}{8} g \sin \theta t^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta) t^2$

$\dfrac{1}{4} \sin \theta = \sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta$

Given $\theta = 45^{\circ}$, we have $\sin 45^{\circ} = \cos 45^{\circ} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. Substituting these values:

$\dfrac{1}{4} = 1 - \mu$

$\mu = 1 - \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75$

We are given $\mu = \dfrac{\alpha}{100}$.

$\dfrac{\alpha}{100} = 0.75 \Rightarrow \alpha = 75$

Answer: $75$

q: As per given figure, a weightless pulley P is attached on a double inclined frictionless surface. The tension in the string (massless) will be (if g=10 m s-2)
question: As per given figure, a weightless pulley P is attached on a double inclined frictionless surface. The tension in the string (massless) will be (if g=10 m s-2)
solution:
The magnitude of acceleration of both the blocks will be same and is given by

a=4 g sin 60∘-g sin 30∘4+1

=203-55

a=43-1 m s-1

Applying Newton's second law for second block (along the direction parallel to the incline on which it is placed)

T-5=1×a

⇒T=5+43-1

=43+1 N

subject: Physics
q: Force acts for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> on a body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, starting from rest, after which the force ceases and then body describes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in the next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of force will be :
question: Force acts for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> on a body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, starting from rest, after which the force ceases and then body describes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in the next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of force will be :
solution:
For the second case when body is moving with constant velocity, we can write

50 = V × 10

⇒V=5 m s-1

Now this velocity if the final velocity for the first case, therefore

V = 0+a×20

⇒5=a×20

⇒a=14m s-2

Hence, required force will be

F=ma=20×14= 5 N

q: Water is flowing at a speed of 1.5 m s<sup>-1</sup> through a horizontal tube of cross-sectional area 10<sup>-2</sup> m<sup>2</sup><sup> </sup>and you are trying to stop the flow by your palm. Assuming that the water stops immediately after hitting the palm, the minimum force that you must exert should be (density of water = 10<sup>3</sup> kg m<sup>-3</sup>)
question: Water is flowing at a speed of 1.5 m s<sup>-1</sup> through a horizontal tube of cross-sectional area 10<sup>-2</sup> m<sup>2</sup><sup> </sup>and you are trying to stop the flow by your palm. Assuming that the water stops immediately after hitting the palm, the minimum force that you must exert should be (density of water = 10<sup>3</sup> kg m<sup>-3</sup>)
solution:
υ = 1.5 m/s      A = 1 0 - 2 m 2

Now, F = d dt P

            = d dt m υ = υ dm dt

            = A υ · ρ · υ

            = A ρ υ 2

            = 1 0 - 2 × 1 0 0 0 × 1.5 2

            = 1 0 - 2 × 1 0 0 0 × 2.25

            = 22.5 N

question: A block rests on a rough inclined plane making an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the plane is $0.8$. subject: Physics
q: An inclined plane is bent in such a way that the vertical cross-section is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> is in vertical and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> in horizontal direction. If the upper surface of this curved plane is rough with coefficient of friction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo></math> the maximum height in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> at which a stationary block will not slip downward is________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: An inclined plane is bent in such a way that the vertical cross-section is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> is in vertical and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> in horizontal direction. If the upper surface of this curved plane is rough with coefficient of friction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo></math> the maximum height in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> at which a stationary block will not slip downward is________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
At maximum ht. block will experience maximum friction force. Therefore if at this height slope of the tangent is tanθ, then θ=Angle of repose

∴  tanθ=dydx=2x4=x2=0.5

⇒x=1 and therefore y=x24=0.25 m

=25 cm

∴ Answer is 25 cm

(Assuming that x & y in the equation are given in meter)

q: An insect crawls up a hemispherical surface very slowly. The coefficient of friction between the insect and the surface is $1 / 3$. If the line joining the centre of the hemispherical surface to the insect makes an angle $\alpha$ with the vertical, the maximum possible value of $\alpha$ so that the insect does not slip is given by
question: An insect crawls up a hemispherical surface very slowly. The coefficient of friction between the insect and the surface is $1 / 3$. If the line joining the centre of the hemispherical surface to the insect makes an angle $\alpha$ with the vertical, the maximum possible value of $\alpha$ so that the insect does not slip is given by
solution: The insect crawls up the bowl upto a certain height $h$ only till the component of its weight along the bowl is balanced by limiting frictional force. For limiting condition at point $\mathrm{A}$ $R=m g \cos \alpha$ $F_1=m g \sin \alpha$ Dividing eq. (ii) by (i) $\tan \alpha=\frac{1}{\cot \alpha}=\frac{F_1}{R}=\mu\left[\right.$ As $\left.F_1=\mu R\right]$ $\Rightarrow \quad \tan \alpha=\mu=\frac{1}{3}\left[\because \mu=\frac{1}{3}(\right.$ Given $\left.]\right)$ $\therefore \quad \cot \alpha=3$

The energies, lost by the ball, over the parts, PQ and QR, of the track, are equal to each other, and no energy is lost when particle changes direction from PQ to QR.

question:
A point particle of mass m, moves along the uniformly rough track PQR as shown in the figure. The coefficient of friction, between the particle and the rough track equals μ . The particle is released, from rest, from the point P and it comes to rest at a point R. The energies, lost by the ball, over the parts, PQ and QR, of the track, are equal to each other, and no energy is lost when particle changes direction from PQ to QR.
The values of the coefficient of friction μ and the distance x=QR , are respectively close to:
solution:
tan⁡30o=hℓ

ℓ=h3=23 m

WPQ=-μmgℓ or WQR=-μmgx

μmgℓ=μmgx 

 x=ℓ≈3.5m

x=23 m

now according to work energy theorem

 Wall=∆K

mgh-μmgℓ-μmgx=0

h-μℓ-μx=0

2=μℓ+x⇒μ=2ℓ+x=243=123≈0.29

subject: Physics
q: A uniform metal chain of mass m and length L passes over a massless and frictionless pulley. It is released from rest with a part of its length l is hanging on one side and rest of its length L-l  is hanging on the other side of the pulley. At a certain point of time, when l=Lx, the acceleration of the chain is g2. The value of x is _____.
question: A uniform metal chain of mass m and length L passes over a massless and frictionless pulley. It is released from rest with a part of its length l is hanging on one side and rest of its length L-l  is hanging on the other side of the pulley. At a certain point of time, when l=Lx, the acceleration of the chain is g2. The value of x is _____.
solution:
According to the information given in the question, the part of the chain that envelopes the pulley is very small. Therefore, the acceleration of the chain can be written as,

a=m2-m1m2+m1g

Since a=g2

g2=m2-m1m2+m1g⇒g2=L-l-lLg⇒l=L4=Lx

⇒x=4

subject: Physics
q: A disc rotates about its axis of symmetry in a hoizontal plane at a steady rate of $3.5$ revolutions per second. A coin placed at a distance of $1.25 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the axis of rotation remains at rest on the disc. The coefficient of friction between the coin and the disc is $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
question: A disc rotates about its axis of symmetry in a hoizontal plane at a steady rate of $3.5$ revolutions per second. A coin placed at a distance of $1.25 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the axis of rotation remains at rest on the disc. The coefficient of friction between the coin and the disc is $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
solution:
Using, $\mu m g=\frac{m v^2}{r}=m r \omega^2$ $\omega=2 \pi n=2 \pi \times 3.5=7 \pi \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$ Radius, $r=1.25 \mathrm{~cm}=1.25 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$ Coefficient of friction, $\mu=$ ? $$ \mu m g=\frac{m(r \omega)^2}{r}(\because v=r \omega) $$
$$ \begin{gathered} \Rightarrow \quad \mu=\frac{r \omega^2}{g}=\frac{1.25 \times 10^{-2} \times\left(7 \times \frac{22}{7}\right)^2}{10} \\ =\frac{1.25 \times 10^{-2} \times 22^2}{10}=0.6 \end{gathered} $$

subject: Physics
q: A large number (n) of identical beads, each of mass m and radius r are strung on a thin smooth rigid horizontal rod of length L(L≫r) and are at rest at random positions. The rod is mounted between two rigid supports (see figure). If one of the beads is now given a speed v, the average force experienced by each support after a long time is (assume all collisions are elastic):
question: A large number (n) of identical beads, each of mass m and radius r are strung on a thin smooth rigid horizontal rod of length L(L≫r) and are at rest at random positions. The rod is mounted between two rigid supports (see figure). If one of the beads is now given a speed v, the average force experienced by each support after a long time is (assume all collisions are elastic):
solution:
Average time for 1 collision to occur on 1 wall, 
=tavg=average distance travelledspeed=2L-2nrv

⇒tavg=2L-2nrv. Change in momentum in 1 collision =2mv. Average force on each support, favg=2mvtavg=2mv22L-2nr⇒favg=mv2L-2nr.

F=mg=100 N

q: A system to 10 balls each of mass 2 kg are connected via massless and unstretchable string. The system is allowed to slip over the edge of a smooth table as shown in figure. Tension on the string between the 7th  and 8th  ball is _____  N when 6th  ball just leaves the table.
question: A system to 10 balls each of mass 2 kg are connected via massless and unstretchable string. The system is allowed to slip over the edge of a smooth table as shown in figure. Tension on the string between the 7th  and 8th  ball is _____  N when 6th  ball just leaves the table.
solution:
Acceleration,a=Net pulling forceTotal mass

a=6mg10m=6g10=6 m s-2

Considering ball 8,9 & 10 as system, tension between ball 7 & 8 can be written as,

T7,8=3m×a

=3×2×6=36 N

subject: Physics
q: A bullet of mass $4 \mathrm{~g}$ is fired horizontally with a speed of $300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ into $0.8 \mathrm{~kg}$ block of wood at rest on a table. If the coefficient of friction between the block and the table is $0.3$, how far will the block slide approximately?
question: A bullet of mass $4 \mathrm{~g}$ is fired horizontally with a speed of $300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ into $0.8 \mathrm{~kg}$ block of wood at rest on a table. If the coefficient of friction between the block and the table is $0.3$, how far will the block slide approximately?
solution:
Given, $\mathrm{m}_1=4 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{u}_1=300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ $\mathrm{m}_2=0.8 \mathrm{~kg}=800 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{u}_2=0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ From law of conservation of momentum, $\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{u}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{u}_2=\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2$ Let the velocity of combined system $=\mathrm{v} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ then, $4 \times 300+800 \times 0=(800+4) \times v$ $\mathrm{v}=\frac{1200}{804}=1.49 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Now, $\mu=0.3$ (given)
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\mathrm{a}=\mu \mathrm{g} \\
&\mathrm{a}=0.3 \times 10\left(\text { take } \mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right) \\
&=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$
then, from $\mathrm{v}^2=\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{as}$
$$
(1.49)^2=0+2 \times 3 \times s
$$

$\mathrm{s}=\frac{\left(1.49^2\right)}{6} ; \mathrm{s}=\frac{2.22}{6}=0.379 \mathrm{~m}$
